behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is between manifest validation and module loading/execution across modules (loader + manifest validator + importer). A unit test of only manifest parsing can miss accidental eager import side effects. Integration is enough because behavior text is about local plugin directory loading, with no real network/subprocess/OS-only primitive, no AI output, and no multi-agent pipeline; those facts disqualify System/Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp plugin directory with one invalid manifest (missing required field) and an entry module that would leave a detectable side effect if imported (e.g., writes a marker file or mutates a known global). Run the real loader against that directory; assert rejection includes manifest error and assert side effect did not occur.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's `onLoad` hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears when dependency resolver output is handed to lifecycle executor; mocking either side can hide ordering bugs. Unit-testing topological sort alone is insufficient for hook-call order in the real loader path. System rejected because behavior text only requires local module wiring/order, not subprocess/real HTTP/OS-only effects. No AI dependence or multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Build temp plugins A and B with manifests where A depends on B, and hooks append their names to a shared in-process log/file. Run loader and assert observed `onLoad` order is `[B, A]`.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: This is resolver + load-execution interaction: detecting the cycle must prevent both imports/hooks. Unit cycle-detection alone can miss accidental partial loading. Higher levels rejected by behavior text: it concerns dependency metadata and loader decisions in local process, not real external infra, AI variability, or chained agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Create two plugins A and B with circular manifest dependencies. Provide entry modules/hooks with detectable side effects. Run loader; assert cycle error/report is emitted and assert neither plugin module side effects nor `onLoad` calls occurred.

behaviors.description: A plugin's `onLoad` hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly targets ordering across import execution and hook invocation, which crosses module boundaries. A unit test of hook dispatcher alone cannot catch real import-before-hook regressions. System disqualified because this ordering can be proven in-process with local modules/files; no network/subprocess requirement. No LLM dependency or multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Create a plugin entry module that sets `globalThis.__pluginInitialized = true` during import and exports `onLoad` that asserts the flag is already true (or logs timestamp/order). Run loader and assert no assertion failure and order evidence shows import execution happened before `onLoad`.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its `onLoad` hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is in interaction of error handling, dependency graph filtering, and lifecycle scheduling across multiple plugins. Unit tests of try/catch or graph logic alone can miss continuation/skip behavior. System rejected because behavior text is loader control flow in local process without real external infra. Agentic/Workflow rejected: no AI output quality or multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Set up plugins: `Fail` throws in `onLoad`; `Dep` depends on `Fail`; `Indep` has no dependency on `Fail`. Run loader and assert: error for `Fail` is recorded, `Indep.onLoad` is still called, and `Dep.onLoad` is not called.
